steps.title: Draw the action line (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a single flowing line from the crown of the head, through the spine, and into the heel of the lead foot. A walking figure leans very slightly forward — let that lean live in the line.

steps.title: Mark the key joints (pencil)
steps.description: Place small ticks for the shoulders (a wide horizontal), the hips (a narrower horizontal, tilted slightly opposite to the shoulders — this counterbalance is what walking looks like), the knees, and the ankle level.

steps.title: Sketch the legs (pencil)
steps.description: Draw the lead leg: a thigh line angling forward from the hip, bending at the knee, then a shin dropping toward the ground. The back leg pushes off behind — straighter, more vertical, heel rising. The two legs form a wide V shape.

steps.title: Block in the torso and arms (pencil)
steps.description: Rough in the torso as a simple wedge between the shoulder and hip marks. Add the arms swinging — the arm opposite the lead foot swings forward, the other trails back. Each arm is two strokes: upper arm and forearm, bent at the elbow.

steps.title: Add the head and feet (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch a small oval for the head at the top. Add feet: the lead foot is flat or toes-down, the back foot pushes off with a rising heel. These small shapes anchor the figure to the ground.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the torso and lead side
steps.description: Switch to ink. Ink the head, then trace the torso and the lead arm and leg as connected, decisive strokes. Keep the line moving — a walking figure has momentum, and a hesitant ink line undercuts that.

steps.title: Ink the trailing side (ink)
steps.description: Ink the back arm and back leg. Notice the weight: use a slightly heavier line on the grounded foot where the body presses down, and let the airborne back foot trail off with a lighter stroke.
